items.text: Chapter Four
Scientific Classification of Tuta absoluta (Taxonomy)
Introduction

Scientific classification is the system used by biologists to arrange living organisms into hierarchical groups, beginning with broad groups that include large numbers of organisms and gradually narrowing down to the genus and species levels.

Classification is not limited to assigning a name to an insect; it also clarifies its position among living organisms and the degree of its relationship to other species. It also helps in understanding its anatomical characteristics, life cycle, feeding behavior, diagnostic features, and control methods.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the phylum Arthropoda, then to the class Insecta, and subsequently to the order Lepidoptera, which includes moths and butterflies. It is placed within the family Gelechiidae, a group that includes small moths whose larvae commonly feed inside plant tissues.
The species is currently classified under the name:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
While the former name:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
Remains the most widely recognized name in agriculture and applied research.
Complete Scientific Classification

rows: Tuta absoluta or tomato leaf and fruit borer

items.text: The taxonomic hierarchy can be represented briefly as follows:
Animalia
Arthropoda
Hexapoda
Insecta
Lepidoptera
Gelechioidea
Gelechiidae
Gelechiinae
Gnorimoschemini
Phthorimaea
Phthorimaea absoluta
Simplified Explanation of the Taxonomic Levels
Kingdom Animalia
Kingdom: Animalia
Tuta absoluta belongs to the kingdom Animalia because it is a multicellular, heterotrophic organism, meaning that it cannot produce its own food as plants do.

The insect obtains its food from other organisms. During the larval stage, it feeds on plant tissues, especially tomato tissues, whereas its body does not perform photosynthesis or produce organic matter from water and carbon dioxide.
Tuta absoluta shares several general characteristics with other animals, including:

items: Ability to move during one or more stages of the life cycle.
Presence of sensory and nervous systems.

items.text: The animal kingdom includes highly diverse groups such as mammals, birds, fish, mollusks, worms, and insects.
Phylum Arthropoda
Phylum: Arthropoda
The insect belongs to the phylum Arthropoda, one of the largest animal groups in terms of number of species.
Arthropods are characterized by several fundamental features:

items: A body composed of segments or sections.
Development of specialized sensory and nervous systems.

items.text: Exoskeleton
The insect’s body is covered by an exoskeleton composed mainly of chitin. This covering provides:
However, the exoskeleton does not expand continuously, so the larva must molt several times in order to increase its body size.
Jointed Appendages
The name Arthropoda refers to the presence of legs and appendages made of sections connected by joints, allowing precise movement.
The phylum Arthropoda includes:
Although all of these groups are arthropods, Tuta absoluta is an insect and not a mite, spider, or crustacean.
Subphylum Hexapoda
Subphylum: Hexapoda
The term Hexapoda refers to animals that possess three pairs of true legs, that is, six legs.
This characteristic is clearly evident in the adult insect, in which all six legs are attached to the thorax.
The body of the adult insect consists of three main regions:
The thorax bears:

items: Two pairs of wings in the adult insect.

items.text: The larva has three pairs of true thoracic legs in addition to fleshy abdominal prolegs that assist in movement. The abdominal prolegs are not true jointed legs like the thoracic legs.
Class Insecta
Class: Insecta
Tuta absoluta belongs to the class Insecta.
Adult insects are generally characterized by the presence of:

items: A thorax composed of three segments.
An abdomen composed of several segments.
One or two pairs of wings in most adult species.

items.text: Insects differ from mites and spiders in the number of legs and body segmentation. Adult insects have six legs, whereas adult mites and spiders usually have eight legs.
Tuta absoluta undergoes complete metamorphosis during its life cycle and passes through four distinct stages:

The form, behavior, and function of the larva differ greatly from those of the adult insect. The larva is the feeding and damaging stage, whereas the adult is the mating, dispersal, and egg-laying stage.
Order Lepidoptera
Order: Lepidoptera
Tuta absoluta belongs to the order Lepidoptera, which includes moths and butterflies.
The name Lepidoptera is derived from two Greek roots referring to:
This is because the wings of the adult insect are covered with minute microscopic scales.
General Characteristics of Lepidoptera
Insects belonging to this order are characterized by several features, including:

items: Presence of two pairs of wings in the adult.
Wings and body covered with fine scales.
A distinct head in the larva.
Three pairs of thoracic legs in the larva.
Presence of abdominal prolegs in most larvae.
Transformation into a pupa before adult emergence.

items.text: Tuta absoluta belongs to the group of small moths and is not a fly. This clearly distinguishes it from leaf-mining flies of the genus Liriomyza, which belong to the order Diptera.
Why Is It Considered a Moth Rather Than a Diurnal Butterfly?
There are general differences between moths and daytime butterflies, although both belong to the order Lepidoptera.
The moth of Tuta absoluta is characterized by:

items: Greater activity in darkness or low light.
Wing margins provided with long fringes.
Hiding among plant leaves during daylight hours.

items.text: Therefore, the term “Tuta moth” is more entomologically accurate than “Tuta butterfly,” although both expressions are understood in field usage.
Superfamily Gelechioidea
The family to which Tuta absoluta belongs is placed within the superfamily Gelechioidea.
This group includes a large number of small moths, many of which are referred to as micro-moths or:
Microlepidoptera
Describing them as micro-moths does not mean that they are microscopic organisms; it means that the adults are small compared with larger moths and butterflies.
Many species within this group are characterized by:

items: Reliance on microscopic characters for accurate identification.
Larval feeding inside plant tissues or protected plant parts.
Relative specialization on certain plant hosts.

items.text: These characteristics explain why it is difficult to identify Tuta absoluta based on size and color alone, because several small moths may appear externally similar.
Family Gelechiidae
Tuta absoluta belongs to the family Gelechiidae.
This is a large family of small moths, whose members are sometimes called:
Gelechiid moths
The family includes many species associated with plants and agricultural crops.
General Characteristics of the Family
Adults in this family are commonly characterized by:

items: Gray or brown colors that aid camouflage.
Nocturnal activity or activity during periods of low light.

items.text: The larvae of many species are characterized by feeding in protected sites, such as:

items: Between leaves that are rolled or tied with silk.

items.text: This feeding pattern is clearly expressed in Tuta absoluta, whose larvae mine inside tomato leaves and also attack growing points, stems, and fruits.
Agricultural Importance of the Family
The family Gelechiidae includes several important agricultural pests, such as:

items: Tuta absoluta, the tomato leaf and fruit borer.
Other species attacking grains, leaves, seeds, and fruits.

items.text: The presence of Tuta absoluta and potato tuber moth within the same family explains some similarities between them, but it does not mean that they are the same species.
Subfamily Gelechiinae
Tuta absoluta is placed within the subfamily Gelechiinae, one of the internal groups of the family Gelechiidae.
The subfamily level is used to divide large families into smaller groups containing genera and species that share a more specific set of anatomical and evolutionary characteristics.
Differences among subfamilies are not easily observed during field inspection, but are generally based on:

items: Features of the head and antennae.

items.text: Field diagnosis may therefore reach the genus or species level through symptoms, larvae, and traps, whereas precise taxonomic confirmation requires a specialist or laboratory examination.
Tribe Gnorimoschemini
Within the subfamily Gelechiinae, the insect belongs to the tribe:
Gnorimoschemini
The tribe is a taxonomic rank located between the subfamily and genus.
This tribe includes a group of small moths whose larvae are associated with several plants, particularly some species of the family Solanaceae and other herbaceous plants.
Closely related genera within this tribe include:
The close relationship among these genera led to repeated changes in the classification of Tuta absoluta, which was historically transferred among:

The taxonomic revision published in 2021 returned the species to the genus Phthorimaea after morphological analysis showed that it belongs to an evolutionary group that includes Phthorimaea operculella.
Genus Phthorimaea
A genus is a group of closely related species that share more specific anatomical and evolutionary characteristics than those shared at the family or tribe levels.
The insect is currently placed in the genus:
Phthorimaea
This genus includes small moths, several of which are associated with plants of the family Solanaceae.
Among the best-known agricultural species within this genus are:
Phthorimaea absoluta
The tomato leaf and fruit borer, known as Tuta absoluta.
Phthorimaea operculella
The potato tuber moth, an important pest that attacks potato leaves and tubers in the field and in storage.
Relationship Between the Two Species

rows: Leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits

items.text: Both pests may occur in the same agricultural area, particularly where tomato and potato are grown. Therefore, observing a small moth alone is not sufficient to identify the species.
Species Phthorimaea absoluta
The species is the most precise principal level in scientific classification used to identify an organism.
The complete species name is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The first part refers to the genus:
Phthorimaea
While the second part refers to the specific epithet:
absoluta
The word absoluta alone is not written as a complete scientific name because the correct name consists of both the genus name and the specific epithet.
This species is distinguished by an integrated set of characteristics, including:

items: External morphology of the egg, larva, pupa, and moth.
Shape of body and wing scales.
Characteristics of male and female genitalia.
Strong association with tomato and solanaceous hosts.

items.text: Scientific identification of the species is not based on a single symptom, such as the presence of a leaf mine, because several insects can form similar mines.
Why Was the Genus Changed from Tuta to Phthorimaea?
For many years, the species was known as:
Tuta absoluta
However, its position within the tribe Gnorimoschemini remained taxonomically debated because of the similarity among several genera within this group.
The modern taxonomic revision relied on analysis of a set of morphological characters that could be compared among closely related genera.
The analysis showed that the species known as Tuta absoluta falls within a monophyletic group that includes:
Phthorimaea operculella
Which is the type species of the genus Phthorimaea.
Based on this relationship, the species was returned to its original genus, and the name became:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The genus Tuta remained taxonomically valid, but according to the modern revision, it no longer includes this species.
The difference between the two names does not indicate any change in the insect or the nature of its damage, but rather a revision of its position within the taxonomic system.
Simplified Concept of Classification
Scientific classification can be compared to a hierarchical address that precisely identifies the location of the insect.
If the species represents a specifically identified individual, the higher levels resemble:
The lower the level, the more precise the identification becomes.
For Tuta absoluta:

items: Animalia: indicates that it is an animal.
Arthropoda: indicates that it is an arthropod with an exoskeleton.
Hexapoda: indicates that it belongs to the six-legged animals.
Insecta: indicates that it is an insect.
Lepidoptera: indicates that it is a moth or butterfly with scale-covered wings.
Gelechiidae: places it among the small moths belonging to this family.
Gnorimoschemini: associates it with a more specific group of closely related moths.
Phthorimaea absoluta: identifies the species itself individually.

items.text: Classification therefore does not merely involve memorizing Latin names; it progressively clarifies the identity of the insect and its relationship to other organisms.
Taxonomic Difference Between Tuta and Leaf Miner
Taxonomy helps clarify that Tuta absoluta differs fundamentally from leaf-mining flies commonly known in the field as leaf miners.
Both groups are capable of forming mines inside leaves, but similarity in damage does not indicate close taxonomic relationship.
Tuta absoluta is taxonomically closer to the potato tuber moth than to Liriomyza flies, even though both may infest tomato leaves.
Taxonomic Difference Between Tuta and True Weevils
Some growers refer to Tuta as “the weevil,” but taxonomy demonstrates that this name is scientifically incorrect.
Tuta is a lepidopteran insect, whereas true weevils belong to the order Coleoptera.
Importance of Taxonomy in Integrated Pest Management
Identification of the Pheromone
Each species has a specific pheromone composition, and the pheromone of Tuta absoluta is not suitable for monitoring all moths or leaf-mining insects.
Larval Diagnosis
A lepidopteran larva differs from a dipteran larva in:
Selection of Natural Enemies
Some parasitoids and predators are associated with specific groups or life stages. Therefore, correct species identification affects the selection of biological control agents.
Insecticide Resistance Management
Sites of action and effective insecticide groups differ according to pest species. Resistance records and IRAC rotation programs are also based on correct identification of the pest species.
Preventing Confusion Among Traps
Tuta absoluta, potato tuber moth, and other small moth species may occur in the same agricultural area.
Taxonomic diagnosis prevents all moths found in a trap from being interpreted as a single species.
Plant Quarantine
Competent authorities use the scientific name and international code to identify the pest in:
Tuta absoluta carries the EPPO code:
GNORAB
This is a stable code that permits tracking of the pest even when its scientific name changes.
Taxonomy and Field Diagnosis
Routine field inspection does not require memorizing all taxonomic ranks, but it does depend on understanding the group to which the pest belongs.
When diagnosing Tuta absoluta in the field, several indicators are examined:

items: Form of the larva and its head.
Presence of thoracic legs and abdominal prolegs.
Infestation of growing points and stems.

items.text: Specialized taxonomic diagnosis may rely on:

items: Microscopic features of the adult insect.

items.text: Laboratory examination becomes necessary when the pest is detected in a new region or when morphologically similar species are present.
Chapter Summary
Tuta absoluta belongs to the kingdom Animalia, phylum Arthropoda, and class Insecta. It is a moth in the order Lepidoptera and is not a fly or a weevil.
Its complete classification is as follows:
The complete modern scientific name is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The name widely recognized in agriculture is:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
Taxonomy confirms that:

items: Tuta is a moth belonging to Lepidoptera.
Liriomyza leaf miners are flies belonging to Diptera.
True weevils are beetles belonging to Coleoptera.
Potato tuber moth is a species closely related to Tuta within the genus Phthorimaea, but it is not the same species.

items.text: This classification enables precise identification of the pest, prevents confusion with other insects, and links field diagnosis to the appropriate monitoring, control, and plant-quarantine measures.
Key References
